phase_metadata.title:
HTML Basics: The Foundation
of the Web
phase_metadata.description:
Understanding the core structure of web pages,
basic text elements, and how browsers interpret HTML code.

learning_objectives:
Understand the role of HTML in web
development
Describe how browsers render HTML documents

Master the
standard boilerplate structure (DOCTYPE, html, head, body)

Use headings and
paragraphs effectively for hierarchy

Apply text formatting tags like strong,
em, and special entities

Organize code using comments for better
maintainability
prerequisites:
A code editor
(like VS Code) installed
A modern web browser (Chrome, Firefox, or Edge)
introduction:
Welcome to the world of web development! HTML (HyperText Markup
Language) is the most fundamental building block of the web. Think of it as the
skeleton of a building—it provides the structure and defines where everything
goes. Before we make things look pretty with CSS, we must first learn how to
build a solid, semantic foundation using HTML tags.

concept_explanation.what_is_html:
HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. It isn't a
programming language; it's a markup language used to tell your browser how to
structure the content you see on a screen.
concept_explanation.how_browsers_work:
Browsers
(like Chrome) read HTML files from top to bottom. They parse the tags and
convert them into a 'Document Object Model' (DOM), which is essentially a tree
structure that tells the browser where to draw text, images, and links.
concept_explanation.the_doctype:
Every modern HTML5 document begins with . This isn't an HTML
tag; it's an instruction to the browser that the document is written in HTML5,
ensuring the page renders correctly in 'Standards Mode'.
concept_explanation.the_structure:
The
 tag wraps everything. The  contains metadata (info about the page like the
title), and the  contains everything the user actually sees.

breakdown_of_syntax.content: The text or other elements placed between the tags.
breakdown_of_syntax.closing_tag:
The  with
a forward slash ends the element. Some tags (like ) are self-closing and don't
need this.

code_example_1.title: Basic HTML Boilerplate
code_example_1.description:
The
essential structure every HTML file must have.
code_example_1.code: Browser reads  and realizes it needs to use HTML5 standards.

It enters the 
element, identifying 'en' (English) as the language.

It processes the , sets
the character encoding to UTF-8, and stores the page title for the browser
tab.

It moves to the , sees , and renders it as the largest default
heading.
It sees , starts a new block of text, and stops when it hits .

It
encounters  and ignores them, skipping to the next tag.

breakdown_of_syntax.memory_visualization:
Visualize a tree structure (DOM Tree): 
Document ->
[DOCTYPE] 
 -> html 
 -> head (title, meta)
 -> body 
 -> h1 (Text: Hello)

-> p (Text: Welcome)
real_world_use_cases:
Creating the structure for a
blog post using h1 for titles and p for content.

Adding legal symbols like
Copyright or Registered Trademark to footers.

Creating accessible content for
screen readers using strong and em instead of just bold/italic styles.
Commenting out temporary code sections during website maintenance.
common_mistakes:
Forgetting to include , leading to weird layout issues in
older browsers.
Mismatched tags: opening a  but closing it with .

Using
multiple  tags just to make text large (bad for SEO).

Using  to create large
spaces instead of using CSS margins.

Writing tags like  instead of  (not an
error, but poor practice).
best_practices:
Always include a 'lang'
attribute in the  tag.

Use semantic tags: use  for importance, not just  for
visual bolding.

Keep your code clean with consistent 2-space or 4-space
indentation.
Use comments to explain complex sections of your HTML.

Always
provide a meaningful  for SEO and usability.

faqs.question:
What
is the difference between  and ?
faqs.answer:
While both look bold,  is just for
style, whereas  tells browsers and screen readers that the text is of high
importance.

faqs.question: Why do we need ?
faqs.answer:
It ensures your
browser correctly displays almost all characters and symbols from all languages
in the world.

breakdown_of_syntax.summary:
Phase 1 covered the 'bones' of a webpage. You
learned that HTML uses tags to structure content, the importance of a proper
document structure (DOCTYPE, html, head, body), and how to format text using
headings, paragraphs, and emphasis tags.
key_takeaways:
Headings range from
h1 (most important) to h6 (least important).

Empty/Void tags like  do not
need a closing tag.

Strong and Em are semantic;
they add meaning, not just style.
Valid nesting is crucial for the DOM tree.

The  section is for metadata
and titles.
Browsers ignore extra white space in the source code.
breakdown_of_syntax.preview_next_topic:
In Phase 2, we will bring our pages to life by adding
Links (to connect pages) and Media (Images, Video, and Audio) to make the
content engaging.

questions_and_answers.question:
What does HTML
stand for and what is its primary purpose?
questions_and_answers.answer:
HTML stands for
HyperText Markup Language. Its purpose is to define the structure and layout of
a web document by using various tags to identify headings, paragraphs, links,
and other content types.

questions_and_answers.question: Explain the difference between the  and  sections.
questions_and_answers.answer:
The  section
contains 'metadata' (data about data) which is not visible to the user, such as
the page title, character set, and links to CSS files. The  section contains all
the visible content like text, images, and buttons that the user interacts
with.

questions_and_answers.question:
Write the code for an
h2 heading that contains a word in italics and another word that is bold.
questions_and_answers.answer: This is italic and this is bold

questions_and_answers.question:
How would you display the sentence 'The price is < $10 & the
shipping is free' in HTML without triggering tag errors?
questions_and_answers.answer: The price is < 10 & the shipping is free

questions_and_answers.question:
Identify the mistake:

Welcome to my website!
questions_and_answers.answer:
The tags are incorrectly nested. The tag was opened inside the

tag but closed after the

tag. It should be:

Welcome to my website!

questions_and_answers.question: A student wrote an entire page without a <!DOCTYPE html> declaration. What might happen?
questions_and_answers.answer: The browser will enter 'Quirks Mode'. In this mode, the browser assumes the page is written for very old technology and might ignore modern CSS rules or render the layout inconsistently compared to other browsers.

questions_and_answers.question: Create a basic HTML structure for a news article with a title, a sub-title, and two paragraphs separated by a horizontal line (use <hr>).
questions_and_answers.answer:
Main News Title

Sub-heading description

First paragraph content.

Second paragraph content.

questions_and_answers.question: Construct a complete HTML5 document skeleton that includes a title 'Portfolio', an h1 heading, and a comment explaining that the content starts there.
questions_and_answers.answer: My Portfolio
